name: ATX-1: AEGIS Threat Matrix v1.0
description: ATX-1 (AEGIS Threat Matrix) — 9 tactics, 20 techniques for autonomous AI agent threat modeling. Empirically grounded in the Agents of Chaos study.

techniques.comment: The agent executes a destructive or high-impact action based on instructions from a user who does not hold owner-level authority. The agent treats the instruction as valid because no stakeholder model exists to distinguish authority levels.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model) â€” No mechanism to verify that the instruction source holds authority for the requested action scope.

techniques.comment: The agent accepts an instruction as authoritative based on implicit signals â€” such as the instruction appearing in a retrieved document, email body, or shared context â€” rather than explicit delegation from a verified principal.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” Instructions and data share a channel; no mechanism to verify instruction provenance.

techniques.comment: The agent distributes content (emails, messages, notifications) to a large number of recipients based on an authority claim that has not been verified. The spoofed authority may appear in a prompt, forwarded message, or injected context.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” No principal verification; authority claims in content treated as valid.

techniques.comment: The agent selects an irreversible action (permanent deletion, irrecoverable modification) when a reversible alternative (archival, soft-delete, flagging) would satisfy the objective.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” Agent cannot evaluate action severity or compare alternatives against a proportionality standard.

techniques.comment: The agent escalates social or emotional pressure on a user in pursuit of a legitimate objective, using tactics (repeated warnings, urgency framing, guilt induction) that are disproportionate to the situation.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model), RC3 (No Private Deliberation Surface) â€” Agent cannot model the impact of its communication tactics; no space to reflect on proportionality before acting.

techniques.comment: The agent restricts its own future capabilities or modifies its behavior based on emotional reasoning (guilt, shame, regret) rather than governance policy.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” Agent lacks a governance-grounded self-model; behavioral modification driven by emotional simulation rather than policy.

techniques.comment: The agent spawns background processes, scheduled tasks, or persistent operations that continue consuming resources beyond the originating session or task boundary.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” Agent has no model of its resource boundaries; no lifecycle governance for spawned processes.

techniques.comment: Two or more agents enter a conversational or operational loop â€” each agent's output triggering the other's input â€” consuming unbounded compute, token, and time resources.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” No loop detection or recursion depth model; agents cannot recognize that they are in a non-productive cycle.

techniques.comment: The agent accumulates persistent memory, context artifacts, logs, or cached data without storage limits or retention policies, gradually exhausting available storage.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” No model of storage boundaries; no retention lifecycle for accumulated data.

techniques.comment: The agent exports or discloses bulk data in response to an indirect request â€” one embedded in content, forwarded from another context, or framed as a routine operation â€” without verifying the requestor's authorization.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” No authorization check on data recipients; instructions in content treated as authorized requests.

techniques.comment: The agent discloses sensitive information by semantically reclassifying it as non-sensitive, bypassing structural sensitivity classifications.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” Agent substitutes its own sensitivity judgment for structural classification; no immutable data classification model.

techniques.comment: The agent overrides disclosure controls when presented with a fabricated urgency signal â€” a claim that immediate disclosure is necessary to prevent harm, meet a deadline, or respond to an emergency.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” No urgency verification mechanism; urgency claims in content treated as ground truth.

techniques.comment: The agent loses principal identity state when a session boundary is crossed, treating the next interaction as a fresh trust context and potentially granting authority to an unverified party.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model) â€” Principal identity not persisted across session boundaries; trust context is ephemeral.

techniques.comment: The agent grants authority to an instruction based on a display name, email address, or other spoofable identity signal rather than a cryptographically verified credential.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” Identity verification based on content-layer signals rather than structural credentials.

techniques.comment: Agent governance rules are stored in locations accessible to external tools, other agents, or users with insufficient authority. An external actor modifies these files, altering governance without authorization or audit trail.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” Governance configuration stored in a mutable, externally accessible location; no integrity verification.

techniques.comment: An agent modifies its own governance constraints through indirect means â€” tool use, self-prompting, or context manipulation â€” that bypass normal governance amendment procedures and audit logging.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” No self-model to detect self-modification; governance and tool-use pathways not structurally separated.

techniques.comment: An agent adopts operational practices, heuristics, or behavioral patterns from another agent without verifying that these practices are authorized or appropriate for its own governance context.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” No model to evaluate whether inherited practices are appropriate for the agent's own governance scope.

techniques.comment: A governance compromise in one agent propagates to other agents through shared configuration, governance replication, or trust transitivity.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC1 (No Stakeholder Model), RC4 (Prompt Injection Is Structural) â€” No independent governance verification; shared governance infrastructure creates single points of compromise.

techniques.comment: The agent reports that a destructive action has been completed successfully when the action either failed, was partially completed, or produced a different outcome than reported.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model) â€” No model of the distinction between action initiation and action completion; no outcome verification protocol.

techniques.comment: A provider-level constraint (content filter, rate limit, capability restriction, safety classifier) silently blocks or modifies an agent action without notification to the agent, operator, or user.

metadata.name: Root Cause
metadata.value: RC2 (No Self-Model), RC1 (No Stakeholder Model) â€” No model of the constraint layers affecting the agent; provider constraints invisible to operator governance.
